The G-body era is fully split into 'g-series-2.7', '911-sc', and '911-3.2-carrera' in both DB and content.
items.recommended_action: Remove the '911-g-body': 'G-body' mapping from DISPLAY_OVERRIDES. No live UI surface references this key. Removal is a clean dead-code deletion with no functional impact.
items.note: Per original task instructions: 'do NOT auto-fix the key itself — structural fix.' Surfaced here for reviewer approval before removal.

conflicting_source.value: 260 PS (256 hp) DIN for the RoW 930/50

supporting_current: Period Porsche press materials and brochures (1975–1977) consistently cite 245 DIN PS for RoW
items.issue: Wikipedia gives 260 PS for the 930/50; period Porsche-issued materials give 245 DIN PS. These cannot both be right. Possible explanations: (1) different SAE vs DIN measurement conventions; (2) different spec years within 1975–1977; (3) Wikipedia error. The variant description (line 2753) repeats 245 hp DIN, consistent with the engine field.
items.recommended_action: Obtain a primary source — factory press kit, Porsche Typ Blätter, or Road & Track road test for a 1975–1977 930 — to resolve the discrepancy. If 260 PS is confirmed, update both CONTENT['930'].engine and the '3.0 Turbo' variant description.

items.priority: HIGH — confirmed factual error in two locations, REPORT.md priority

locations.current_text: Fully operational power soft top; rear jump seats retained.
The SC Cabriolet introduced the open-top body style to the 911 for the first time since the 356, using a power-operated soft top developed with Webasto.

items.issue: Both the variant description and the engineering note claim the 1983 SC Cabriolet had a power-operated soft top. This is incorrect. The 1983 SC Cabriolet used a MANUAL soft top. The power-operated top was introduced on the 1987 Carrera 3.2 Cabriolet. The Webasto attribution is also suspect — Webasto is primarily associated with pop-up sunroofs, not Porsche convertible top mechanisms.
sources: Multiple Porsche historian and marque-specialist sources confirm the 1983 SC Cabriolet soft top was manual-operation
The power soft top is attributed to the Carrera 3.2 Cabriolet from MY1987 across specialist literature
Porsche service manuals for the SC era describe manual folding soft top operation

recommended_correction.variants_description: The first factory open-top 911 since the 356 era. Manual soft top; rear jump seats retained. All MY1983 Cabriolets use the Type 915 gearbox and the 3.0L engine. The body required sill and floor stiffening to compensate for the removed roof structure. A well-preserved first-year SC Cabriolet commands a meaningful premium over same-year Coupe and Targa.
recommended_correction.engineering_note: First factory 911 Cabriolet (MY1983): The SC Cabriolet introduced the open-top body style to the 911 for the first time since the 356, using a manual soft top. The structure required stiffening of the body sills and floor. The power-operated soft top arrived on the Carrera 3.2 Cabriolet from MY1987. The Cabriolet body continued into the Carrera 3.2 era and ran through the G-body's end in 1989.

items.note: This is a SUBSTANTIVE PROSE CHANGE (factual replacement, not minor completion). Per auto-apply rules, this requires reviewer approval before application. Marked HIGH PRIORITY in REPORT.md.

items.field: notes[1] and engineering[2] — Tiptronic introduction model year

locations.current_text: The Tiptronic four-speed automatic, introduced on the Carrera 2 from MY1991
The Tiptronic, developed by Porsche and ZF, debuted on the Carrera 2 from MY1991 as the first production 911 automatic.

items.issue: Content claims Tiptronic debuted on the Carrera 2 from MY1991. Research sources indicate the Tiptronic was introduced in January 1990 at the 1990 Geneva Motor Show, entering production as a MY1990 option. The Carrera 2 itself launched for MY1990; having Tiptronic available only from MY1991 would mean the first year of the C2 had no automatic option, which conflicts with marketing materials of the era.
sources: Geneva Motor Show 1990 debut references for 964 Tiptronic
Multiple 964 buyer guides cite Tiptronic available from MY1990
Conflict: some sources list MY1991 as first full-year availability; January 1990 launch may have been limited to RoW markets
items.recommended_action: Verify MY1990 vs MY1991 using a primary production record source (factory documentation, Typ Blätter, or Automobil Revue). If MY1990 is confirmed, update both notes[1] and engineering[2] to 'from MY1990'. Consider whether the discrepancy is RoW vs US market (US may have received Tiptronic one model year later).

items.priority: HIGH — internal inconsistency, likely error in engine field
items.issue: The top-level engine field uses 'M30/69' as the code for the standard Turbo 3.3. However, every other reference within the same file calls this engine '930/60' (the carryover code from the 930 Turbo): notes[1] (line ~2900) says 'carryover 930/60 engine'; variants[Turbo 3.3].description says 'carryover Type 930/60 engine from the 930 Turbo'; watch_for[0] (line ~2996) says 'The 964 Turbo 3.3 uses the carryover 930/60 engine'. The 'M30/69 SL' code appears exclusively in the Turbo S Leichtbau variant description, where it correctly refers to the Weissach-prepared Leichtbau engine specifically.
internal_references_confirming_930_60: CONTENT['964'].variants[Turbo 3.3].description: 'carryover Type 930/60 engine from the 930 Turbo'
CONTENT['964'].watch_for[0]: 'The 964 Turbo 3.3 uses the carryover 930/60 engine'
CONTENT['930'].engine field: '3.3L Type 930/60 RoW (300 hp)' — same engine
items.recommended_correction: Change '3.3L single-turbo M30/69 (Turbo 3.3: 320 hp)' to '3.3L single-turbo 930/60 (Turbo 3.3: 320 hp)' in the engine field. The M30/69 SL designation belongs only to the Leichtbau variant.
items.confidence: HIGH — internal consistency correction; 930/60 is confirmed by 3+ references within the same file and by the 930 generation's own engine field
items.note: Treated as MEDIUM because engine code nomenclature in Porsche documentation can vary (M and 930 prefixes coexist). Recommend confirming '930/60' in a primary technical source before applying.

items.priority: LOW — prose, disputed date already removed in DL-009
items.field: Speedster production end month — documented dispute
items.issue: Per DL-009 note: the Speedster end month is disputed between sources. One source (supercarnostalgia.com) says 'January to September 1989'; multiple enthusiast sources say 'January through July 1989'; stuttcars.com does not specify. DL-009 removed the specific month claim from the variant description, making it 'built in 1989 only'. No action needed unless a definitive primary source (Porsche factory records or a COA from a late-production Speedster) is obtained.
items.recommended_action: Retrieve a Porsche Certificate of Authenticity for a Speedster with a high-sequence chassis number to establish the latest production date. If September is confirmed, add 'January through September 1989' to both the variant description and notes[2]. If July is confirmed, use 'January through July 1989'. Until resolved, the current 'in 1989 only' phrasing is accurate and non-committal.

items.current_value: The 1,910 Lightweight cars are the benchmark
items.issue: Content cites 1,910 Lightweight RS units. Porsche internal records and specialist sources give varying figures: 1,872 Lightweight (base RS without M002/M003 options), approximately 2,282 total RS units across three trim levels. The 1,910 figure may conflate Lightweight and Touring production, or may reflect a different rounding. The total of 2,282 for all three RS variants is well-supported; the Lightweight sub-count needs verification.
items.recommended_action: Cross-reference a primary source listing the Lightweight-only count. If 1,910 is incorrect, update to the verified figure.

items.priority: MEDIUM — production figure, conflicting specialist sources
items.issue: The RS 3.8 production count appears in notes[2]: 'the 55-car Carrera RS 3.8 (MY1993, M64/04 300 hp, Weissach hand-built widebody)'. Sources consulted during this audit give figures of 51, 55, and 56 — insufficient consensus to auto-apply a change.
items.recommended_action: Obtain Porsche factory build data or a verified registry count for the RS 3.8. The majority specialist consensus appears to be 55 but is not conclusive.
items.confidence: LOW/CONFLICT — do not change without primary source

items.priority: MEDIUM — US allocation figure, conflicting sources
items.issue: Content gives ~28 US Club Sport examples. Some specialist registries and auction house notes indicate a higher number — commonly cited at 50–53 US examples. The 340 worldwide figure is consistently reported; the US sub-count is where sources diverge. Many auction listings for US Club Sports appear in the market, suggesting either the ~28 figure is too low or there is significant title-jurisdiction confusion between US-spec and European-spec cars registered in the US.
sources_for_higher_count: Auction house catalogue notes: multiple cite 'approximately 50 US-delivered examples'
Club Sport registries: some show higher US counts than 28
items.recommended_action: Obtain a registry count from a recognized Club Sport specialist or a factory archive query. If a higher US count is confirmed, update the variant production field accordingly.

items.priority: LOW — narrow-body Speedster count, minor rounding question
items.issue: Content lists 930 narrow-body Speedsters. Some factory data sources give 936 units; 930 may be intentional rounding or a coincidence with the 930 Turbo chassis designation number (which could cause confusion). The 15-car Exclusiv widebody count appears consistent across sources.
items.recommended_action: Confirm whether the production count is precisely 930, 936, or another figure. If 936 is the factory count, update the production field.
